title: Access Restrictions for Change — Privileged Route Changes

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 CM-5 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 CM-5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.55: Access Restrictions for Change — Privileged Route Changes.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.55' running on cisco:ios routing syslog messages, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Network device TAs (Cisco IOS, Juniper), syslog
spl:
index=net sourcetype=cisco:ios earliest=-7d
| search "%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE" OR "%SYS-5-CONFIG_I" OR "neighbor"
| stats count by user, host, command
| where count>0 AND NOT user IN ("svc_net_automation")
description: Monitors BGP or routing table changes from privileged accounts for CM-5 access restrictions for change.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes net and sourcetypes cisco:ios. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Enable AAA accounting to syslog; (2) parse `user` field; (3) alert on human-initiated changes without ticket keyword in command comment if used; (4) integrate RANCID/Oxidized diffs optional. Indexes required: `index=net`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cisco:ios`.
knownFalsePositives: Config changes during scheduled maintenance, controlled deployments, or platform upgrades.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Network device TAs (Cisco IOS, Juniper), syslog.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `cisco:ios` routing syslog messages.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Enable AAA accounting to syslog; (2) parse `user` field; (3) alert on human-initiated changes without ticket keyword in command comment if used; (4) integrate RANCID/Oxidized diffs optional.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=net sourcetype=cisco:ios earliest=-7d
| search "%BGP-5-ADJCHANGE" OR "%SYS-5-CONFIG_I" OR "neighbor"
| stats count by user, host, command
| where count>0 AND NOT user IN ("svc_net_automation")
```

Understanding this SPL

**Access Restrictions for Change — Privileged Route Changes (CM-5)** — Monitors BGP or routing table changes from privileged accounts for CM-5 access restrictions for change.

Documented **Data sources**: `cisco:ios` routing syslog messages. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Network device TAs (Cisco IOS, Juniper), syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: net; **sourcetype**: cisco:ios. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=net, sourcetype=cisco:ios, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user, host, command** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count>0 AND NOT user IN ("svc_net_automation")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare a sample of the same events in the source system (domain controller, cloud tenant, or device) the security team would open first. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (commands), Timeline, Single value (human changes)
grandmaExplanation: We watch BGP or routing table changes from privileged accounts for CM-5 access restrictions for change. That helps federal and agency security reviews match what the systems actually do day to day.
